question: Describe the process of performing an in-order traversal on a binary tree. What is the order of node visitation, and what is one common use case for this type of traversal?
expectedPoints: Visits the left subtree, then the root, then the right subtree (LNR).
Results in nodes being visited in ascending order for a Binary Search Tree.
Common use case: retrieving sorted data from a BST.

question: Explain the algorithm for deleting a node from a Binary Search Tree (BST) that has two children. Why is this case more complex than deleting a leaf node or a node with one child?
expectedPoints: Find the in-order successor (smallest in the right subtree) or predecessor (largest in the left subtree).
Copy the value of the successor/predecessor to the node targeted for deletion.
Recursively delete the successor/predecessor node from its original position.
Complexity arises from needing to maintain the BST property (left child < parent < right child) after the removal.

question: Compare and contrast the time complexity of the core operations (search, insertion, deletion) on a Binary Search Tree in the best-case and worst-case scenarios. What structural property of the tree defines these scenarios, and what data structure can be used to guarantee the better performance?
expectedPoints: Best-case (balanced tree): O(log n) for search, insertion, and deletion due to halving the search space.
Worst-case (degenerated linked list/skewed tree): O(n) for all operations, as it must traverse each node.
The height of the tree, specifically whether it is balanced or not, defines the scenario.
Self-balancing trees (e.g., AVL, Red-Black) use rotation algorithms to maintain balance and guarantee O(log n) performance.
